# Introduction: The vantage, the questions, the method {#sec-intro}

> *A claim to test, not a creed to defend.*

## The view from the other shore

Imagine the world economy not from London or New York but from a beach on the Malabar
coast, or the deck of a dhow running before the monsoon from Aden to Gujarat. It is a
deliberately awkward place to stand, because almost every long-run history of the world
economy is written from somewhere else — from the North Atlantic, looking outward, with
Europe as the active party and the rest of the world as the stage on which European energy
plays out. Tell the story that way and its shape is foreordained: the world is poor and
static until, around 1500 or 1750, Europe stirs, and modern prosperity radiates from a
single source.

Stand on the Indian Ocean shore instead and a different shape appears. You notice that for
most of recorded history the **largest, richest, most commercially sophisticated economies
were in Asia** — Han and Song China, the Abbasid caliphate, Mughal India — and that the
flow of the world's most coveted store of value, silver and gold, ran *toward* them, not
away. You notice that Europe spent long centuries as a **periphery**: an exporter of slaves,
furs and raw metal, an importer of manufactures and ideas, paying for what it wanted in
bullion it could not itself produce in quantity. And you notice that the period in which
Europe genuinely ran the world economy is **short** — concentrated in the century or so
after 1750 — and that even at its height it rested on the Asian periphery it governed.

This book takes that view seriously enough to build a whole history around it. But the
vantage point is an **engine, not a slogan**. The argument it generates is a claim — and the
job of the book is to *test* that claim, period by period, against what the evidence will
actually bear, and to be candid about where the evidence is thin and where serious scholars
disagree.

## The spine: the claim we are testing

Here is the argument in one paragraph. It is worth reading twice, because the rest of the
book is an extended interrogation of it:

> *Viewed from the Indian Ocean, the world economy spent most of its history centred on
> Asia — visible in the direction bullion flowed and in where the world's goods were made.
> Integration advanced and retreated in waves shaped by climate, disease, technology and
> money, along a durable maritime channel and a periodically resurgent overland one. Europe's
> dominance was real and transformative but **bounded** — concentrated in the century after
> 1750, built partly on the Asian centrality it overturned, and resting at its height on the
> Indian Ocean periphery it governed. Its timing and depth remain debated, and the East's
> aggregate return — measurable but partial, achieved within a Western-built order — is the
> long arc closing, not simply repeating.*

Call this **the spine**. Its most provocative move is to treat European dominance as an
**interruption** rather than a destination — but a *bounded* interruption, calibrated rather
than triumphalist. Four qualifications keep that honest, and they recur throughout the book:

1. the interruption was **consequential** — roughly two transformative centuries, not a blip;
2. its **timing and depth are genuinely open** — the crux of @sec-ch07, where the
   Great Divergence is debated;
3. the eventual return east is **partial** — aggregate weight, *not* a restoration of
   per-capita parity; and
4. it is **system-dependent** — the Asian re-rise happened *within* a US-anchored order.

We do not settle the spine in this Introduction. We assemble the tools to test it, and we
return to weigh it in the @sec-conclusion.

## The four questions

The book asks the **same four questions of every period**. They are how each chapter is
organised, and they are the analytic toolkit the book is trying to hand you. Together they
answer the spine — *where is the centre of gravity, and is it moving?* — without
pre-judging it.

::: {.callout-important}
## The four motivating questions
**Q1 — Direction.** Is the system *integrating or disintegrating*? (Two refinements the
evidence forces on the simple cycle: *integration breeds fragility* — the links that carry
goods carry shocks; and *disintegration has winners* — a fracturing core can loosen the
grip on a periphery.)

**Q2 — Channels.** *Which routes* carry the integrating weight — **maritime** or **overland**
— and are they complements or substitutes? (The maritime Indian Ocean is the durable artery;
the overland corridor had **one** true climax, the Mongol Pax of @sec-ch04.)

**Q3 — Modes.** *Which of the four flows* — **goods, technology, people, capital** — is doing
the integrating? Here lies the book's key analytic move: treat **bullion as a *good* until the
nineteenth century, then as *money/capital* after** — and use its direction as the running
tracer of the centre of gravity.

**Q4 — Europe.** What is *Europe's role* — peripheral, entrant, dominant, one of several cores
— and how does it change? Asked as an open question, not assumed.
:::

The spine is answered jointly by **Q3** (follow the bullion, then the capital) and **Q4**
(Europe's changing role); **Q1** and **Q2** describe *how* the system that holds the centre is
wired at each moment. The arc the four questions trace, chapter by chapter, is this:

> no single centre (Ch 1) $\to$ Asia the production-centre of a multipolar relay (Ch 2) $\to$
> Asia central, Europe peripheral (Ch 3) $\to$ Asia central, a first north-west-European seed
> (Ch 4) $\to$ Asia the silver sink (Ch 5) $\to$ Asia the workshop, Europe armed (Ch 6) $\to$
> **the hinge: the centre moves west (Ch 7)** $\to$ peak Western dominance on an Indian-Ocean
> base (Ch 8) $\to$ the order cracks within the West (Ch 9) $\to$ the return toward Asia (Ch 10).

### The bullion tracer, and why it flips

The reason to follow bullion is that, before modern capital markets, **metal moved toward the
workshop**. People who made things the rest of the world wanted — Indian cotton, Chinese silk
and porcelain — were paid in silver and gold, which therefore pooled where production was most
valued. So in antiquity Roman silver drained *east* to India (@sec-ch02); in the early-modern
centuries the New World's silver flowed *east* to China (@sec-ch05). The tracer even works
in reverse where it should: the Abbasid core paid silver *north* to its slave-and-fur periphery
(@sec-ch03), mirroring Rome.

After the nineteenth century the logic flips, because bullion's economic role changes. Under
the classical gold standard and integrated capital markets, gold becomes **money/capital**, and
following it leads not to the workshop but to the **financial core** — London, then New York.
But the book insists you note *who underwrote that core*: India's colonial "drain" helped
finance Britain's payments deficits (@sec-ch08). Sourced in the periphery, intermediated by
the core.

## Six threads that run the length of the book

Beyond the four questions, six **cross-cutting threads** are set up here and paid off later.

1. **Centre of gravity** — the spine itself, tracked with an explicit *evidence ledger* entry
   and a stated **confidence level** in every chapter's verdict.
2. **Measurement of success: quantity to quality.** This is the book's quiet antidote to a
   recurring error. Before about 1800, like every other species, humans turned prosperity into
   **numbers** — more people, more total output, bigger empires (aggregate, *extensive* growth).
   Only after about 1800 did any society turn it into **sustained income per head** (per-capita,
   *intensive* growth — the escape from the Malthusian ceiling, uniquely modern). The thread is
   set up in @sec-ch01, made the centrepiece of the silver story in @sec-ch05 and
   @sec-ch06, and resolves at @sec-ch07: **the Great Divergence *is* this switch.** It is
   what stops us reading every "thriving" empire — Han, Song, Mughal, Qing — as if it were
   growing in the modern sense.
3. **Follow the silver** — the early-modern operationalisation of the bullion tracer
   (@sec-ch05 to @sec-ch07): the same American silver met different monetary conditions
   and so had different fates — Asia absorbed and specialised, the Ottomans inflated, West
   Africa debased and depleted, and **Europe converted its monetary "loss" into armed-trade and
   financial power** — the bridge to the divergence.
4. **Measurement of integration — how we know.** The evidence available to measure integration
   itself improves over time: from the mere **diffusion** of goods and ideas, to **bullion
   flows**, to **wages and prices**, to **GDP**, to a computed centre of gravity. Before prices,
   only the diffusion prong exists — which constrains what @sec-ch01 and @sec-ch02 can
   claim.
5. **Forces.** Four drivers sit behind Q1's direction — **climate, disease, technology,
   policy** — two of them natural, a reminder that the world economy has always been embedded
   in a natural system.
6. **Data survival and bias.** What survives reflects record-keeping conventions and the
   accidents of climate and preservation. **Absence of data is not absence of activity**, and
   the surviving record systematically under-documents the very Indian-Ocean world this book
   centres on. Whose eyes we are seeing through is itself a running question.

## A note on method, and on honesty

Two methodological commitments follow from all this, and they are why the subtitle is *data
and debates*.

**Data.** The book leans on numbers — trade volumes, bullion flows, real wages, output shares,
the computed economic centre of gravity — because numbers are how you adjudicate a claim like
the spine rather than merely asserting it. The figures and tables are **reproducible**:
generated from datasets you can download and re-run. But the book is equally insistent that
the data thin out dramatically as you go back, and that they are biased toward the literate,
fiscal, European end of the record. A chapter on the Bronze Age and a chapter on the twentieth
century are *not* equally evidenced, and the book will not pretend otherwise.

**Debates.** Where the scholarship is genuinely contested — *when* the Great Divergence began
and *why*; whether interwar disintegration was man-made or inevitable; when "globalisation"
really started — the book stages the disagreement rather than smuggling in a verdict. Disputed
figures are presented as teaching points, not silently resolved. The aim is not to tell you
what to think about the world economy, but to hand you the questions, the evidence and the
live arguments, and let you test the spine yourself.

::: {.callout-note}
## How to read the chapters
Each chapter opens by **following one object** that carries its period's story, locates the
period **on the arc**, sets out **the cast** and **how we know**, narrates **the period on its
own terms**, then reads it through **the four questions**, delivers a **centre-of-gravity
verdict** with a confidence level, and hands off to the next. Look out for the recurring
**Data exhibit** and **Debate**, and the **Follow the money** and **How we know** callouts.
:::
